items.prompt_md: In the k-closest-points heap, why is the heap keyed on `-dist` (negative squared distance) instead of the raw distance?
options: Negation makes heapq run faster
It turns heapq's min-heap into a max-heap by distance, so the root is always the current farthest of the k kept so far
It avoids floating-point rounding errors
heapq requires all keys to be negative
items.answer: It turns heapq's min-heap into a max-heap by distance, so the root is always the current farthest of the k kept so far
items.explanation_md: You need to evict the farthest survivor when the heap overflows past k, so the root must represent the largest distance — negation makes that true under heapq's min-heap semantics.

items.prompt_md: What does the root of the size-k max-heap (by distance) represent once the heap has grown past k points?
options: The closest point overall
The farthest of the k closest points found so far — the boundary that any new point must beat to get in
An arbitrary point with no special meaning
The average distance of all points seen
items.answer: The farthest of the k closest points found so far — the boundary that any new point must beat to get in
items.explanation_md: Since the heap holds exactly the current k closest, its root (the max by distance) is the worst survivor — the cutoff a new point must beat to displace it.

items.prompt_md: Why does the k-closest solution compare squared distance (`x*x + y*y`) rather than the true Euclidean distance with a square root?
options: Squared distance is required by heapq syntax
Square root and squared distance preserve the same relative ordering, so skipping the root saves work without changing which points are closest
Squared distance is always smaller so it fits in a heap better
It has nothing to do with correctness or performance — it's just a style choice
items.answer: Square root and squared distance preserve the same relative ordering, so skipping the root saves work without changing which points are closest
items.explanation_md: Square root is monotonic for non-negative inputs, so comparing squared distances gives identical ordering to comparing true distances, avoiding needless sqrt calls.

items.prompt_md: A new point arrives whose distance is farther than the current root of the size-k max-heap (by distance). What happens under the standard push-then-trim code?
options: It gets pushed and then immediately popped back off, since it isn't among the current k closest
It silently gets ignored without touching the heap
It replaces the root directly without any heap operation
It forces a full re-heapify of all k elements
items.answer: It gets pushed and then immediately popped back off, since it isn't among the current k closest
items.explanation_md: Pushing a farther point makes it the new max (by distance); since the heap now exceeds size k, popping removes exactly that point again — net effect: no change to the k-closest set.

items.prompt_md: Why does sorting all n points by distance and slicing the first k do more work than strictly necessary?
options: Sorting is always slower than any heap operation
A full sort establishes the relative order of all n points, but the problem only needs to know which k are closest, not their order among themselves
Sorting cannot handle negative distances
Sorting requires more memory than any heap could ever use
items.answer: A full sort establishes the relative order of all n points, but the problem only needs to know which k are closest, not their order among themselves
items.explanation_md: Full ordering is extra information the task doesn't ask for — a size-k heap tracks only the boundary needed to know membership in the closest-k set.

items.prompt_md: What is a common bug when adapting the top-k running-maximum heap idea to 'k closest' problems?
options: Using a size-k heap at all
Getting the polarity backwards — using a min-heap on raw distance (which tracks the k farthest) instead of a max-heap on distance (which tracks the k closest)
Computing distance as x*x + y*y
Returning a list instead of a set
items.answer: Getting the polarity backwards — using a min-heap on raw distance (which tracks the k farthest) instead of a max-heap on distance (which tracks the k closest)
items.explanation_md: For 'k closest' you must evict the farthest survivor on overflow, which requires the root to be the max distance — a plain min-heap on distance does the opposite job.

items.prompt_md: Why must the heap in k-closest be explicitly bounded to size k inside the loop (popping whenever it exceeds k), rather than just pushing every point and sorting at the end?
options: Without the bound, the heap grows to size n, losing the O(log k) per-operation cost and O(k) memory that make the approach worthwhile
Popping is required syntactically by heapq even if size doesn't matter
The bound has no effect on complexity, only on output order
Bounding is only needed when k equals n
items.answer: Without the bound, the heap grows to size n, losing the O(log k) per-operation cost and O(k) memory that make the approach worthwhile
items.explanation_md: The entire benefit of the size-k heap comes from capping its size — an unbounded heap degenerates into the same cost profile as a full sort.

items.prompt_md: How would you adapt the k-closest heap to instead return the k **farthest** points from the origin?
options: No change is needed — the same code already returns the farthest points
Flip the polarity: use a min-heap keyed on raw distance, bounded to size k, evicting the closest survivor on overflow
Use a hash map keyed by distance value
Sort ascending and take the first k, same as for closest
items.answer: Flip the polarity: use a min-heap keyed on raw distance, bounded to size k, evicting the closest survivor on overflow
items.explanation_md: For k farthest you need to evict the current closest survivor on overflow, so the root must be the minimum distance — a plain min-heap on raw distance does exactly that.

items.prompt_md: Why is it fine for k-closest to store the raw coordinates `(x, y)` alongside `-dist` in each heap entry, rather than just the distance?
options: It isn't fine — only the distance should ever be stored
The heap only compares the first tuple element for ordering, so tacking on `(x, y)` lets you recover which point the distance belongs to without affecting heap order
Storing coordinates makes the heap operations faster
heapq requires exactly three elements per tuple
items.answer: The heap only compares the first tuple element for ordering, so tacking on `(x, y)` lets you recover which point the distance belongs to without affecting heap order
items.explanation_md: Python tuple comparison is lexicographic, so as long as `-dist` is first and distinct enough to decide ordering, the trailing coordinates just ride along for later retrieval.

items.prompt_md: What is the overall time complexity of the size-k heap approach to finding the k closest points among n points?
options: O(n)
O(n log k)
O(n log n)
O(k log n)
items.answer: O(n log k)
items.explanation_md: Each of the n points does O(log k) of heap work (push, and possibly a pop), since the heap never exceeds size k.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of fully sorting all n points by distance and slicing the first k?
options: O(n)
O(n log k)
O(n log n)
O(k)
items.answer: O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: A full sort orders all n points regardless of k, costing O(n log n) even though only k of them are ultimately needed.

items.prompt_md: n = 1,000,000 points and k = 5. Roughly how do the size-k heap and full-sort approaches compare?
options: They're the same, since both touch every point once
The heap approach is much cheaper: O(n log k) with a tiny log k versus O(n log n) with a much larger log n
The full sort is actually cheaper here because k is so small
Neither approach's complexity depends on k or n
items.answer: The heap approach is much cheaper: O(n log k) with a tiny log k versus O(n log n) with a much larger log n
items.explanation_md: With k this much smaller than n, log k is a small constant compared to log n, so the heap approach's per-element cost is far lower.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the size-k heap approach to k-closest, in terms of k and n?
options: O(k)
O(n)
O(n + k)
items.answer: O(k)
items.explanation_md: The heap never holds more than k entries at a time, so its memory footprint is bounded by k regardless of how large n grows.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of a single `heappush` or `heappop` call on a heap that is bounded to size k?
options: O(log k)
O(k)
O(log n)
items.answer: O(log k)
items.explanation_md: Heap operations cost time proportional to the log of the heap's current size, and that size never exceeds k here.

items.prompt_md: Given a list of 200,000 cities with coordinates, return the 8 cities closest to a given reference point.
options: Sort all 200,000 cities by distance and take the first 8
Maintain a size-8 max-heap keyed by distance, evicting the farthest survivor whenever it grows past 8
Build a hash map of city name to coordinates
Use two pointers scanning from both ends of the city list
items.answer: Maintain a size-8 max-heap keyed by distance, evicting the farthest survivor whenever it grows past 8
items.explanation_md: 'k closest, k much smaller than n' is the trigger for a bounded size-k heap keyed on the computed distance.

items.prompt_md: You need the 3 employees whose performance scores are closest to a target score of 85, out of a workforce of 50,000.
options: Keep a size-3 max-heap keyed by |score - 85|, evicting the farthest survivor on overflow
Sort all 50,000 employees by raw score
Count how many employees have each exact score in a hash map
Use two pointers over an unsorted score list
items.answer: Keep a size-3 max-heap keyed by |score - 85|, evicting the farthest survivor on overflow
items.explanation_md: The metric here is distance-from-a-target, and only 3 of 50,000 are needed — exactly the size-k heap-by-distance pattern.

items.prompt_md: A GPS app must show the 5 nearest gas stations to the driver's current location, streaming in station data one at a time.
options: Sort the full stream once all stations have arrived
Maintain a size-5 max-heap keyed by distance to the driver, evicting the farthest whenever the heap exceeds 5
Build a hash map keyed by station name
Use BFS outward from the driver's location
items.answer: Maintain a size-5 max-heap keyed by distance to the driver, evicting the farthest whenever the heap exceeds 5
items.explanation_md: A bounded 'k nearest under a stream' query is the size-k heap pattern, whether the data arrives all at once or incrementally.

items.prompt_md: From a dataset of 2 million product prices, find the 10 prices closest to $49.99.
options: Maintain a size-10 max-heap keyed by |price - 49.99|, evicting the farthest survivor on overflow
Sort all 2 million prices and slice near the middle
Hash map counting how many products cost exactly $49.99
Two pointers from the min and max price
items.answer: Maintain a size-10 max-heap keyed by |price - 49.99|, evicting the farthest survivor on overflow
items.explanation_md: Distance-from-a-target plus a small fixed k relative to a huge n is the signature of the size-k heap-by-distance approach.

items.prompt_md: You must find the k points in a 2D plane with the smallest Euclidean distance to the origin, where k is fixed and n (total points) can be huge.
options: Fully sort every point by distance and take the first k
Maintain a size-k max-heap keyed by squared distance, evicting the farthest survivor past k
Build a hash map keyed by (x, y) coordinate pairs
Use two pointers converging from opposite ends of the point list
items.answer: Maintain a size-k max-heap keyed by squared distance, evicting the farthest survivor past k
items.explanation_md: This is exactly the k-closest-points setup: bounded heap by distance beats a full sort when k is much smaller than n.

items.prompt_md: A recommendation engine wants the 20 movies whose runtime is closest to the user's preferred 110 minutes, out of a catalog of 500,000 movies.
options: Sort all 500,000 movies by runtime and binary-search for the middle
Maintain a size-20 max-heap keyed by |runtime - 110|, evicting the farthest survivor on overflow
Hash map counting movies by exact runtime value
Use a stack to track movies as they're scanned
items.answer: Maintain a size-20 max-heap keyed by |runtime - 110|, evicting the farthest survivor on overflow
items.explanation_md: Wanting a bounded k subset ranked by closeness to a target value, with k tiny compared to n, is the size-k heap-by-distance trigger.

items.prompt_md: Given an unsorted array, count how many times each distinct value appears — no ranking or 'closest' notion is involved.
options: Maintain a size-k heap keyed by value
Build a hash map that counts occurrences of each value in one pass
Sort the array and scan for the k closest values to zero
Use two pointers from both ends of the array
items.answer: Build a hash map that counts occurrences of each value in one pass
items.explanation_md: There's no distance metric or bounded top-k selection here, just exact frequency counts — a hash map is the direct tool.

items.prompt_md: Two sorted arrays of numbers must be merged into a single sorted array.
options: Maintain a size-k max-heap keyed by distance from a target
Use two pointers, one per array, always advancing the one with the smaller current value
Build a hash map counting values across both arrays
Use a stack to reverse each array before merging
items.answer: Use two pointers, one per array, always advancing the one with the smaller current value
items.explanation_md: Merging two already-sorted sequences is the classic two-pointers trigger; there is no 'k closest' selection to justify a heap.

items.prompt_md: Determine whether a string of brackets like `"([{}])"` is validly balanced.
options: Maintain a size-k heap of bracket positions
Push opening brackets onto a stack and pop-match them against closing brackets
Build a hash map of bracket-to-count
Use two pointers from each end of the string
items.answer: Push opening brackets onto a stack and pop-match them against closing brackets
items.explanation_md: Nested, last-opened-first-closed matching is the stack pattern's signature; there's no distance or ranking involved, so a heap doesn't apply.

items.prompt_md: From a live feed of earthquake readings, you must always be able to report the 6 epicenters currently closest to a fixed monitoring station.
options: Re-sort the entire history of readings every time a report is requested
Maintain a size-6 max-heap keyed by distance to the station, evicting the farthest survivor whenever the heap exceeds 6
Build a hash map counting readings per epicenter id
Use two pointers over the readings sorted by timestamp
items.answer: Maintain a size-6 max-heap keyed by distance to the station, evicting the farthest survivor whenever the heap exceeds 6
items.explanation_md: A live, bounded 'k closest to a fixed point' query under a streaming feed is the size-k heap-by-distance pattern, not a full re-sort.
